spec_match.rationale: Emits the correct ttl=<seconds> parameter on Use-As-Dictionary, demonstrates the cache-variant blow-up the chromestatus motivation cites, and ties the convergence directly to ttl as the lever.

interactive.rationale: Three sliders (deploys/week, ttl, stale-user share) plus a Recalculate button drive an 8-week timeline of dictionary lanes, a four-stat meter (live / expired / peak / savings), and a live header preview.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index sells exactly 'the variant set converges with ttl=' and the page shows that convergence happening in the meter and timeline visualizations.

edge_cases.rationale: Includes ttl=0 (immediately-expires) on the slider and shows zero savings in that case. Missing: the case where stale-user share is high but ttl is low (CDN keeps the variant cached because some user still asks for it — does ttl actually bound CDN-side cache pollution?), and the per-dictionary id reuse pattern (when id stays the same across deploys, the variant problem disappears).

references.rationale: Links chromestatus.com/feature/6408307086589952 AND the IETF draft draft-ietf-httpbis-compression-dictionary — best references list of the four sibling concepts.

design_system.rationale: All via CSS variables; uses color-mix() to derive a faded --accent-rose for expired cells while keeping AA contrast with --bg-paper; no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Surface the id-reuse alternative
openQuestions.detail: Compression Dictionary Transport lets you ship a stable id across deploys (e.g. id='app') instead of bumping the id each release. That's the OTHER lever for keeping variants bounded — and it's invisible here. Add a toggle 'stable id vs new id per deploy' so authors see that ttl is one of two convergence strategies.

openQuestions.title: Show CDN-side variant lifetime, not just client-side
openQuestions.detail: The stat meter conflates 'browser still has the dictionary' with 'CDN still caches the variant'. They're different: the CDN's variant set is driven by the Vary: Available-Dictionary header and the CDN's own cache policy, not by client ttl alone. A small panel that tracks 'CDN variants remembered' separately from 'clients with live dictionaries' would clarify which side ttl actually bounds.

openQuestions.title: Add a 'stale-user with expired dict' replay simulation
openQuestions.detail: When ttl expires client-side, the spec says the browser stops sending Available-Dictionary. The simulator already shows variants going 'expired' but doesn't replay what those users now request — they should appear in the served bar as plain-brotli requests. Show that flow so the cliff between fresh and expired is visible per request, not just per variant.

summary: Best of the four — correct ttl= header, both spec references, real timeline + meter UI. Two follow-ups would lift it: contrast ttl with the id-reuse strategy (the other lever), and split CDN-side variant retention from client-side dictionary retention.
